topics.title: Super Bowl Analysis and Halftime Show Critique
topics.summary: The studio reviews the Super Bowl game, discussing betting outcomes, the Rolling Stones' halftime performance, and various musical acts while reacting to fan celebrations.

topics.title: Super Bowl Commercials and Musical Performances
topics.summary: The studio critiques various Super Bowl commercials, discusses the salaries of sports officials, and evaluates a live musical performance by Train.

topics.title: Tributes to Grandpa Al Lewis
topics.summary: The studio pays tribute to the late actor Grandpa Al Lewis, discussing his iconic role in The Munsters, his history as a basketball scout, and his impact on comedy.

topics.title: Tributes to Grandpa Al Lewis
topics.summary: The studio discusses the legacy of Grandpa Al Lewis, his appearances on the show, and the upcoming tribute segments while also touching on Super Bowl officiating and recent program highlights.

topics.title: Dave Chappelle's Career Decisions
topics.summary: The studio discusses Dave Chappelle’s decision to walk away from a lucrative contract with Comedy Central and his subsequent appearance on Oprah Winfrey.

topics.title: Dave Chappelle's Departure from Comedy Central
topics.summary: The studio discusses Dave Chappelle's reasons for leaving his show on Comedy Central, exploring themes of creative freedom, the pressures of success, and potential internal conflicts with production staff.

topics.title: Collective Soul Performance and Songwriting
topics.summary: The studio hosts members of Collective Soul to discuss their independent record label, the creation of hit songs like "Breakaway," and their upbringing in a religious household.

topics.title: Collective Soul Performance and Super Bowl Review
topics.summary: The studio hosts members of Collective Soul to discuss their musical inspirations, the nuances of performing acoustic sets, and recent performances by The Rolling Stones at the Super Bowl.

topics.title: Collective Soul Interview and Dispute Over Loan
topics.summary: The studio hosts an interview with Collective Soul members before transitioning into a heated dispute between former employees over a loan and a watch used as collateral.

topics.title: Casey's Claims Regarding Debt and Sexual Encounters
topics.summary: The studio features a heated dispute between Casey and Lori over a debt, the possession of a watch, and conflicting claims regarding sexual encounters involving Jessica.

topics.title: Casey and Laurie Dispute
topics.summary: The studio hosts a heated confrontation between Casey and Laurie over an unpaid debt and allegations regarding a watch.

topics.title: Super Bowl Lingerie Ball and Upcoming Show Topics
topics.summary: The studio discusses the Super Bowl lingerie ball, the intersection of sports and sex, and previews upcoming segments regarding personal success, guest appearances, and the definition of a sports fan.

topics.title: Congressman Ackerman Presentation
topics.summary: The studio hosts Congressman Gary L. Ackerman, who presents a flag flown over the U.S. Capitol in honor of Howard Stern's debut on Sirius Satellite Radio.

topics.title: Memorial for Barry Koussel
topics.summary: The studio announces a memorial service and celebration for Barry Koussel on February 18th in Newport, Rhode Island.

topics.title: Radio Programming and Celebrity News
topics.summary: The studio discusses upcoming radio programming, the technical difficulties of 3D television, and various celebrity news stories involving high-profile figures.

topics.title: Super Bowl Commercials and Media News
topics.summary: The studio reviews Super Bowl advertisements, celebrity news regarding Oprah Winfrey and Courtney Love, international incidents involving a Danish cartoon and an Egyptian ferry accident, and legal updates concerning Robert Blake.

- That was not a championship game, that was a game of like two mediocre teams playing.
- Why do you say that?
You always say stuff like that.
- No, I was just saying because they played so poorly.
- They were two well-matched teams, that's why.
- I didn't think they were well-matched.
I thought it was a very sloppy game.
- You don't think defense was strong on both sides?
- No, in fact, even that pass that Roethlisberger threw, when it was like third and 25, it was
a crappy pass that should have been knocked down.
It just didn't seem like a well-played game.
- He didn't have a great game.
I think he completed nine passes total, Roethlisberger.
He didn't have an awesome game.
He didn't even know who to pick for MVP because nobody really had a standout game.
- That is key when you have a game like that and nobody knows who the MVP should be.
- I think it's so funny that--
- Who was the MVP?
- Heinz Ward.
- Heinz Ward.
But it's so funny that the Steelers have a guy named Heinz on it.
No one talks about that enough.
- Let me talk about the Rolling Stones.
I disagree with Gary on this.
Gary says it was the worst halftime show in the history of the Super Bowl.
- Well, I just thought that the Stones sounded awful.
- All right, first of all, technically, the first song that they did--
I'm talking about the engineers didn't even have a guitar on.
It wasn't the Stones' fault.
That's number one.
I sat there and I said, "I guess it doesn't matter what the game is, what the network is.
Engineers cannot get it right ever."
- I said the same thing.
I mean, how long did they have to get ready for this?
- I mean, months and months and months.
And the Stones come on and Mick Jagger's voice is--
You can hear Mick Jagger's voice and he has no guitars.
I don't think Keith Richards' guitar or Ron Wood's guitar was up.
- You know, I rolled back the tape 'cause I said, "Was that a false start?
What happened there?"
- It was a bad start, but not on the Stones' fault.
Again, engineering, technical difficulties.
- Yeah, it wasn't them.
- But even when they--
- You always think they get this together at the last minute.
- But even when the mix was right, I thought they sounded terrible.
- Now, here's the other thing.
Mick Jagger-- The Stones were very good.
I even liked the new song they did. I was shocked.
- I did, too.
- I was like, "Oh, no, here comes a new song."
I didn't think the new song was bad.
- I was digging it. Now I'm like, "Wait a minute. This is a new song."
- And what is the name of that new song, Fred? Do you know?
- No, I don't, but it actually sounds like an old song.
- It did. Well, that's why it was good.
- It does. It sounds like a throwback.
- One of the newer ones that you didn't like.
- I wish I knew the name. I'd play it.
- I had just seen them in concert a couple weeks ago and they sounded so great.
So compared to that, I didn't love it.
Yeah, I mean, compared to them live a couple of weeks ago, my God.
They were so amazing live.
- I thought the biggest faux pas of the halftime show was Mick Jagger's decision
to wear a belly shirt. There is nothing more disturbing than seeing a guy in his 60s.
Granted, the guy's in great shape, but he loves that belly.
He loves that midriff like a Britney Spears or a teenage girl.
This guy parades around in that belly shirt.
And every time he lifts his arms up intentionally, he knows what he's doing, a guy.
He's very aware of his self-image.
Every time he lifts that shirt up and he's showing that belly, I'm like, "What are you, a chick?"
- See, I thought it was the shaking of the 60-year-old hips.
- No, no. - That does not work.
- That didn't work. - It was the arms.
- The jelly on the arms.
- When he takes that move and whips off the jacket -- and by the way, the guy's not that warm.
It's Detroit. It's outdoors.
He's got to whip off that jacket, you know, and he's showing --
- He's still doing it. He's doing what he did when he was 20.
- He's showing off those arms. And, yeah, they're toned, but that's 60-something-year-old skin.
And you see the underside of the arms flapping around.
It's not a good look.
I say lose the belly shirt, lose the tight shirts, and --
- Don't shake your hips.
- And the stones at the end, when they took their final bow,
turns out a couple of the guys in the band -- not Charlie Watts, of course,
but a couple of guys in the band -- were all wearing belly shirts.
- Really? - I didn't notice that.
- Any guy in a belly shirt, I'm not into looking at.
- You bet.
- And I'm in a 60-year-old. Come on.
- Yeah, I mean, you know --
- So you'd rather look at a 20-year-old already?
- I'm saying any guy. I just said it.
- All right, if it's Ricky Martin, I'll look.
By the way --
- By the way, though, I did think, rather than having all these weird people
running up on stage together, it was great to just have a block of the stones.
- Right.
Rolling stones in a single is called "Rough Justice."
See if you got that in that profit system.
- Take a gander.
- Is that what it's called, the profit system?
- Yeah.
- See, I'm bringing you into our inner workings -- profit system.
Rough justice. It sounded okay.
I mean, I was like, "Wow, you know, I hate --"
I went, "Oh, no, the Stones are doing a new song.
Everyone hates that.
Don't they know they're not going to sell any albums?"
And then I get to admit, the song was in bed.
- Yeah.
- This is it?
- Supposedly.
- What do you mean, "supposedly"?
It says "Rough Justice" on it, right?
- Oh, that was the information we were getting.
This doesn't sound like it, though, is it?
- Maybe it is.
- Not bad.
- Like, I don't even recognize this as a song from last night.
- I don't either.
- I mean, you're probably right, but --
- I don't remember it.
I remember not being offended by it and kind of into watching them do it.
And I was shocked.
- Did you see -- I guess they're on delay.
And I guess Mick got bleeped last night.
The guys were telling me.
- Oh, really?
- Maybe because he said, "Cock."
- No, he said, "You make a grown man come," or something like that there,
and start me up.
And the guys were just telling me out there.
I didn't even notice.
- No, I think he actually censored himself.
- I think self-edited.
- That's the lyric is, "You can make a dead man come."
- Right.
- "You make a dead man come."
- Yeah, that's the actual lyric in the song.
- So I have to say also -- this is just some of the showbiz stuff.
- But did you -- you were about to comment on whether it was a good or bad halftime show.
- It was a good halftime show.
The Stones did a good job.
Aside from the technical screw-up, the new song wasn't offensive.
The belly shirt was a faux pas.
The arms flapping was a faux pas.
But the music sounded good.
It was good to see the Stones.
For a guy like me, anytime I could see the Stones, I'm into it.
It was good, and Mick Jagger still can dance around and entertain a crowd,
and he's got a full head of hair.
- Yeah, but it reminded me of when you'd see Aerosmith,
and then the next thing you know, Jessica Simpson's coming up through the floor
and Britney Spears is -- I was like, those were horrible halftime shows.
- This was good.
- Yeah, it was good.
- This was good.
It was the Stones.
I liked the tongue thing retracting.
That was kind of cool.
- That was great.
- What did you think of the beginning?
Did you see --
- Yeah, so I'm going to comment on next.
The next musical performance I will comment on,
and I don't know if J.D. pulled this for me.
I know he's sitting there watching TV all morning.
Aretha Franklin and Aaron Neville.
And Dr. John, I believe it was?
- Yeah.
- Dr. John looks like he needs a doctor.
He is no doctor.
He needs to self-medicate.
The guy's on his last legs.
Dr. John -- Aretha Franklin looks like she should have suited up
and gotten right in the game for Seattle.
She should have been a linesman.
I'd never seen such girth and boos.
- That was the buzz, wasn't it?
- Yeah, I mean, unbelievable.
And Aaron Neville -- we still ask,
"What is that thing on Aaron Neville's face?"
And we should be playing that song.
We're entitled to it.
- I was going to say, it reminded me of the song.
- Yeah.
That's Aaron Neville's face song we can play.
- Yep.
- Where is that?
We got in the profit system?
- I've got to look.
- It's got to be there, right?
- Not everything's over yet.
We're still in the process of bringing stuff over.
- You've got to be kidding me.
Fred, you got that?
- I'll take a look.
I think that's one of those things that Gary's right,
because we're still transferring.
We're also creating new stuff at the same time.
- Hey, by the way, talk about crazy bets, Will was just telling me,
he bet the under over on the national anthem
at two and a half minutes and he won.
It came in at 2.15.
- Well, first of all, Richard Huff said it in the Daily News.
It's true.
You could tell that Aretha Franklin and Aaron Neville
did not get together beforehand and rehearse.
- And they didn't get together during the song?
- That's right.
Aaron Neville looked like he was trying to jump in with Aretha Franklin.
- And you can't do that.
- Right.
- He was totally blown off the field.
- Nobody sat there and worked his head ahead of time.
I thought there was poor preparation.
I'm going to give that a C minus,
although Aretha Franklin still has a great voice.
- But she's not even trying to act thin anymore.
- She's suited up and gotten in for Seattle.
By the way, some of those football players are massively fat.
- Yes, yes.
- Who's that one guy on Pittsburgh who can just run also?
- The guy that danced, he's an interior lineman.
I can't remember his name, but he had a sack.
- Jervicious or something?
- No, Jervicious is an end on Seattle.
- Jerome, first of all...
- Bettis.
- Jerome Bettis.
- You're talking about the bus.
- The bus?
- Yeah.
- Boy, that guy can move.
- That guy's not a lineman.
He's a runner.
- Wow.
- He's the running back.
- How do you run that much or be that fat?
- I don't know.
It's one of these things where that guy looks like an out-of-shape bouncer at a strip club.
And he is just one of the best running backs of the last generation.
- All right.
Because I'm one of these guys who's so out of it with sports, answer this question.
- Okay.
- How did ABC end up with the Super Bowl?
Fox and CBS all year long have all the games.
I never see a game on ABC.
Now all of a sudden the Super Bowl's on ABC?
- Well, Monday Night Football.
- Yeah.
They're always in the Monday night.
- I believe that they won't have it next year.
But I think that you can bid on the Super Bowl separate away from owning football.
In other words, the NFL puts that up for bid.
And when the PBS paid enough money, they could get it.
- Whatever happened to Al Michaels appearing on the show?
I guess he's not really that into us anymore.
- Well, he actually called us right before we left Terrestrial Radio.
- Right.
- And we passed on it because we were busy.
- Oh, okay.
All right.
- I mean, we could have had him on.
- Okay, good.
I think for the record, it could have been way worse for the Seahawks.
That interception by Kelly Herndon, that 76-yard interception, that was huge.
Without that, I think it could have been a way bigger blowout.
That was a big break, they got, you know?
- All right.
- I think the Steelers solidly dominated the game.
- You want to hear some Steelers fans reacting?
- All right.
- Number one, I guess it's on the news carts.
- Robin's news page?
- Yeah.
- All right, here we go.
- Here's some excited people.
- All right.
Let me put this up.
- People in the mud now!
- Whoo!
- Whoo!
- One more in the mud!
- When I hear that, "Whoo!"
I just want to just hide.
I just know something bad's going to happen.
- And that one for the towel.
- No, one for the thumb.
- Oh, is that what they're saying?
- Yeah, you know what?
They won four Super Bowls in the late '70s and '80s.
So in '81, their theme was one for the thumb in '81.
So this is now they finally got their one--
- They finally got the one for the thumb.
- Right, now they got five.
- I don't even--I still don't understand.
- Yeah.
- A ring.
- Oh.
- Whoo!
- I think that's the same sound the Nazis made as they marched the Jews into the ovens.
Whoo!
I hear, "Whoo!"
I just got this too much action.
Give me a way.
- Way too much excitement.
- But let me get back to the Pittsburgh fans.
- Go buzzy! Go buzzy! Go Hommies! Go Hommies! Go Hommies! Go Hommies!
Go Hommies! Go Hommies!
- Whoo!
- They sound like kids.
- Oh.
- Who is this, Hamid Karzah?
- Oh, no, no, that's not--you don't want to have to do all the news cards.
I just had one--
- I'll play them all.
- --dealers fan.
- Tell me to play them. I'll play them.
- Reaction.
- All right.
All right, the other thing is, of course, the discussion about the commercials.
I do want to mention that quarterback, Hasselback, or whatever his name is,
he looks like Scott DePace, our director.
- Really?
- Yeah, our TV director for On Demand.
- I don't realize that--
- I'm sorry for him.
- I don't realize Hasselback's father was on the '83 Raiders.
He's got a ring, Hasselback's father. That's a football family.
- All right, let's go to the commercials, because a lot is made of these
commercials only because they spend so much friggin' money.
First of all, what a disappointment that, like, pretty girls can't be in
commercials anymore on the Super Bowl because of the Janet Jackson incident
and because broadcasters cower to the FCC, and they're just so goddamn afraid
of the FCC.
- So were there any good commercials?
I didn't really notice anything but the GoDaddy commercial,
and you couldn't hardly see that?
- The GoDaddy commercial was--I was listening to Taz and Cole on our station,
on Howard 100, and Taz and Cole had on the GoDaddy girl,
and she claims they had to have that commercial edited 14 times before ABC
would allow it on the air.
- They submitted it a number of times. I know that.
- Yeah, but I think the first four they submit on purpose,
hoping that they'll get rejected because it gets in publicity.
- Right.
- But then it got to a point where I think they actually were trying to get
a commercial on it.
- The other one that they made a big hoopla about was Jessica Simpson,
where those bites are made for walking, which makes no--those bites are made
for eating or--I mean, first of all, she mutilated that song,
"These Boots Are Made For Walking," when she did it the first time.
Now she took it to a commercial and did "Those Bites Are Made For Eating"
for little mini pizzas.
She was neither sexy nor a good singer,
and she was the only other sort of hot chick,
and she didn't even look that hot, right?
- I mean, you know, she's always good-looking to me,
but that commercial just was it, huh?
- It was bad.
The good commercials--I'll mention one.
The FedEx commercial with the dinosaur, with the dinosaur boss,
and the Great Effects, that's why that was good,
and the caveman gets stomped on. Excellent.
The other thing was the Leonard Nimoy commercial was good,
although Nimoy looks like an alien at this point.
- Yeah, he looks like Spock.
- He's got 19-year-old teeth. He's got big, fake white teeth.
- He's old, man. Oof.
- Real old, but coming out to the Star Trek convention and being able to do
"Live Long and Prosper" because he now was on some kind of pain medication
was a good idea. I thought that was funny.
I was watching the promo for "Lost,"
and I've got to say that the biggest asshole of the world is Michelle Rodriguez,
who finally gets some work on a good television show and is coming off well,
screws up by going on some sort of weird drinking binge or some kind of weird
rampage out in Hawaii where they film "Lost,"
and now they're talking about getting rid of her.
- Yeah, well, she's a major league F-up.
- Yeah, she's a pain in the ass.
- Every time something good happens for her, she has to make a mess of it.
- What did she do? Did she get in some kind of car accident?
- She was in DWI.
- Her and one of the other women on the show got arrested within an hour of each other.
- She's a screwball.
- That one, too, looks like she doesn't ever want anything.
- Right.
- You know, she's someone, though, we should have back now that we're on Saturday.
- Oh, I've tried to have her back.
I think the publicists have figured out to keep her away from this show.
I'm trying to hold on to that.
- You know what? Let's get her back.
- I tried.
- I don't care. Make it happen.
- They should keep her away from the bottle.
- Because you know what happened was, when she was on our show at K-Rock,
she cursed up a storm.
